examples.explanation:

Step 2: Initialize gcd_val=1
Step 3: Loop i from 1 to 12:
  i=1: 12%1=0, 18%1=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=1
  i=2: 12%2=0, 18%2=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=2
  i=3: 12%3=0, 18%3=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=3
  i=4: 12%4=0, 18%4=2 → not common divisor
  i=5: 12%5=2, 18%5=3 → not common divisor
  i=6: 12%6=0, 18%6=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=6
  i=7: 12%7=5, 18%7=4 → not common divisor
  i=8: 12%8=4, 18%8=2 → not common divisor
  i=9: 12%9=3, 18%9=0 → not common divisor
  i=10: 12%10=2, 18%10=8 → not common divisor
  i=11: 12%11=1, 18%11=7 → not common divisor
  i=12: 12%12=0, 18%12=6 → not common divisor
Step 4: Return gcd_val=6

Step 1: a=7, b=13, smaller=min(7,13)=7
Step 2: Initialize gcd_val=1
Step 3: Loop i from 1 to 7:
  i=1: 7%1=0, 13%1=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=1
  i=2: 7%2=1, 13%2=1 → not common divisor
  i=3: 7%3=1, 13%3=1 → not common divisor
  i=4: 7%4=3, 13%4=1 → not common divisor
  i=5: 7%5=2, 13%5=3 → not common divisor
  i=6: 7%6=1, 13%6=1 → not common divisor
  i=7: 7%7=0, 13%7=6 → not common divisor
Step 4: Return gcd_val=1

approaches.name: Brute Force O(min(a,b))
approaches.summary: Iterate from 1 to the smaller number, checking each integer to see if it divides both numbers evenly, and track the largest such divisor. Use this for educational purposes to understand the problem basics, but it's inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force GCD Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of GCD using brute force iteration from 1 to min(a,b)
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of two numbers a and b works by systematically checking every integer from 1 up to the smaller of the two numbers. The intuition is simple: since the GCD cannot exceed the smaller number, we only need to check divisors up to that limit. We iterate through all possible divisors, test if each divides both numbers evenly (using the modulo operator), and keep track of the largest divisor that satisfies this condition. This approach is straightforward and educational for understanding the fundamental definition of GCD, but it becomes inefficient for large numbers due to its linear time complexity. The algorithm begins by handling zero inputs explicitly: if a == 0, return b; if b == 0, return a. Then it identifies the smaller number between a and b. Next it initializes a variable to store the current maximum GCD found. It loops from 1 to the smaller number inclusive. For each candidate divisor i, it checks if a % i == 0 AND b % i == 0. If both conditions are true, i is a common divisor, so we update our maximum GCD to i. After completing the loop, the stored value is the GCD. Time complexity is O(min(a,b)) because we perform a constant amount of work for each integer up to the smaller number. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, working solution. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting the loop from 0 (which causes division by zero), 2) Using <= instead of < in loop conditions (missing the smaller number itself), 3) Not handling zero inputs, 4) Not handling negative numbers (GCD is defined for positive integers, so take absolute values first), 5) Forgetting to update the GCD variable when a larger divisor is found. Real code pattern: def gcd_brute_force(a, b): a, b = abs(a), abs(b); if a == 0: return b; if b == 0: return a; smaller = min(a, b); gcd_val = 1; for i in range(1, smaller+1): if a % i == 0 and b % i == 0: gcd_val = i; return gcd_val

Step 2: Initialize gcd_val=1
Step 3: Loop i from 1 to 12:
  i=1: 12%1=0, 18%1=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=1
  i=2: 12%2=0, 18%2=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=2
  i=3: 12%3=0, 18%3=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=3
  i=4: 12%4=0, 18%4=2 → not common divisor
  i=5: 12%5=2, 18%5=3 → not common divisor
  i=6: 12%6=0, 18%6=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=6
  i=7: 12%7=5, 18%7=4 → not common divisor
  i=8: 12%8=4, 18%8=2 → not common divisor
  i=9: 12%9=3, 18%9=0 → not common divisor
  i=10: 12%10=2, 18%10=8 → not common divisor
  i=11: 12%11=1, 18%11=7 → not common divisor
  i=12: 12%12=0, 18%12=6 → not common divisor
Step 4: Return gcd_val=6

Step 1: a=7, b=13, smaller=min(7,13)=7
Step 2: Initialize gcd_val=1
Step 3: Loop i from 1 to 7:
  i=1: 7%1=0, 13%1=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=1
  i=2: 7%2=1, 13%2=1 → not common divisor
  i=3: 7%3=1, 13%3=1 → not common divisor
  i=4: 7%4=3, 13%4=1 → not common divisor
  i=5: 7%5=2, 13%5=3 → not common divisor
  i=6: 7%6=1, 13%6=1 → not common divisor
  i=7: 7%7=0, 13%7=6 → not common divisor
Step 4: Return gcd_val=1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Common Divisors
ladders.desc: Check if a number divides two given numbers evenly
ladders.explanation: The key sub-routine in the brute force GCD approach is determining whether a candidate integer i is a common divisor of two numbers a and b. This is the core checking mechanism that runs repeatedly in the loop. The intuition is based on the definition of divisibility: a number d divides another number n evenly if n divided by d leaves no remainder. Mathematically, d divides n if n % d == 0. For i to be a common divisor of both a and b, it must satisfy a % i == 0 AND b % i == 0 simultaneously. This check is fundamental because it directly tests the GCD condition: the GCD is the largest number that divides both inputs without remainder. The algorithm for this sub-routine is straightforward: take three inputs (a, b, and the candidate divisor i), compute the remainders a % i and b % i, and return True if both remainders are zero, otherwise return False. Time complexity is O(1) since it involves only constant-time arithmetic operations. Space complexity is O(1) as it uses a fixed amount of memory. This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by being the conditional check inside the main loop—each iteration uses this to test whether the current i should update the GCD value. Common mistakes include: 1) Using integer division (/) instead of modulo (%) to check divisibility, 2) Not handling the case where i=0 (which would cause division by zero), 3) Checking a % i == 0 OR b % i == 0 instead of AND (which would incorrectly identify numbers that divide only one input), 4) Forgetting to take absolute values of a and b first when dealing with negative inputs. Real code pattern: def is_common_divisor(a, b, i): return a % i == 0 and b % i == 0

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute a % i = 12 % 6 = 0
Step 2: Compute b % i = 18 % 6 = 0
Step 3: Check if both remainders are 0: 0 == 0 and 0 == 0 → True
Step 4: Return True

Step 1: Compute a % i = 12 % 4 = 0
Step 2: Compute b % i = 18 % 4 = 2
Step 3: Check if both remainders are 0: 0 == 0 is True, but 2 == 0 is False → False
Step 4: Return False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Divisor Checking
ladders.desc: Loop through all possible divisors up to the smaller number
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force GCD approach is the iterative process that examines every integer from 1 to the smaller of the two input numbers to find common divisors. The intuition behind this iteration is exhaustive search: since we don't know the GCD in advance, we systematically test all possible candidates within the reasonable range (1 to min(a,b)). This works because the GCD, by definition, must be a divisor of both numbers, and no divisor can be larger than the smaller number. The algorithm involves: 1) Determine the smaller number between a and b (call it 'smaller'), 2) Initialize a variable to track the current maximum GCD found (start at 1, since 1 always divides any integer), 3) Loop through i from 1 to smaller (inclusive), 4) For each i, check if it divides both a and b evenly (using the modulo operation), 5) If it does, update the maximum GCD to i, 6) After the loop completes, the maximum GCD found is the answer. Time complexity is O(min(a,b)) because the loop runs 'smaller' times, with constant work per iteration. Space complexity is O(1). This logic is the central technique that implements the brute force strategy—it connects to the full problem by providing the main control flow that orchestrates the divisor checks. Without this iteration, we'd only check a single divisor rather than finding the maximum. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting the loop from 0 (causes division by zero error), 2) Using exclusive upper bound (i < smaller) instead of inclusive (i <= smaller), which would miss cases where the smaller number itself is the GCD, 3) Not initializing the GCD variable properly (should start at 1, not 0), 4) Updating the GCD variable incorrectly (e.g., setting it to i without checking if i is larger than current GCD—though in ascending order this isn't needed, but it's good practice). Real code pattern: smaller = min(a, b); gcd_val = 1; for i in range(1, smaller+1): if is_common_divisor(a, b, i): gcd_val = i

examples.trace:
Step 1: smaller = min(12,8) = 8
Step 2: gcd_val = 1
Step 3: Loop i from 1 to 8:
  i=1: 12%1=0, 8%1=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=1
  i=2: 12%2=0, 8%2=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=2
  i=3: 12%3=0, 8%3=2 → not common divisor
  i=4: 12%4=0, 8%4=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=4
  i=5: 12%5=2, 8%5=3 → not common divisor
  i=6: 12%6=0, 8%6=2 → not common divisor
  i=7: 12%7=5, 8%7=1 → not common divisor
  i=8: 12%8=4, 8%8=0 → not common divisor
Step 4: After loop, gcd_val=4

Step 1: smaller = min(9,6) = 6
Step 2: gcd_val = 1
Step 3: Loop i from 1 to 6:
  i=1: 9%1=0, 6%1=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=1
  i=2: 9%2=1, 6%2=0 → not common divisor
  i=3: 9%3=0, 6%3=0 → common divisor, gcd_val=3
  i=4: 9%4=1, 6%4=2 → not common divisor
  i=5: 9%5=4, 6%5=1 → not common divisor
  i=6: 9%6=3, 6%6=0 → not common divisor
Step 4: After loop, gcd_val=3

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding the Smaller of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Determine which of two numbers is smaller to set loop bounds
ladders.explanation: A crucial building block for the brute force GCD algorithm is identifying the smaller of the two input numbers a and b. This is necessary because the GCD cannot exceed the smaller number—if we tried to check divisors larger than the smaller number, they couldn't possibly divide it evenly (except in the trivial case where the smaller number is 0). The intuition is based on basic number theory: any divisor of a number n must be ≤ n. Therefore, when finding common divisors of a and b, we only need to check up to min(a,b). The algorithm is simple: compare a and b using a comparison operator (e.g., < or >), and return the smaller value. In pseudocode: if a < b then return a else return b. Alternatively, use a min() function if available. Time complexity is O(1) since it's a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This building block feeds into the core logic by providing the upper bound for the iteration loop—without correctly identifying the smaller number, the loop might run unnecessarily long (if we used the larger number) or miss potential divisors (if we used an arbitrary bound). It connects to the overall problem by optimizing the search space: we avoid checking numbers that cannot possibly be divisors. Common mistakes include: 1) Using the larger number as the bound (which wastes time checking impossible divisors), 2) Forgetting to handle negative numbers (GCD is typically defined for non-negative integers, so take absolute values first), 3) Incorrect comparison when numbers are equal (should return either, as min(x,x)=x), 4) Not considering edge cases where one number is 0 (min(0,x)=0, but GCD(0,x)=x, so need special handling in full problem). Real code pattern: smaller = a if a < b else b

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare a=12 and b=18
Step 2: Check if 12 < 18: True
Step 3: Since True, smaller = a = 12
Step 4: Return 12

Step 1: Compare a=25 and b=10
Step 2: Check if 25 < 10: False
Step 3: Since False, smaller = b = 10
Step 4: Return 10

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo (Remainder) Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation, denoted as a % b in programming, is a fundamental arithmetic operation used extensively in the brute force GCD approach. It calculates the remainder when integer a is divided by integer b. The intuition is that if a % b == 0, then b divides a evenly (i.e., a is a multiple of b). This is crucial for checking divisibility: to determine if a number i is a divisor of a, we compute a % i and see if the result is zero. The algorithm for modulo can be understood as: given integers a and b (with b ≠ 0), repeatedly subtract b from a until the result is less than b; the final result is the remainder. More formally, a % b = a - (b * floor(a/b)). Time complexity is O(1) in practice for fixed-size integers, though theoretically it depends on the division algorithm. Space complexity is O(1). This operation is the basic building block for the divisor checking sub-routine—without modulo, we couldn't efficiently test divisibility. It connects to higher levels by enabling the condition checks in the loop. In the context of GCD, we use modulo twice: a % i and b % i, to check if i divides both numbers. Common mistakes include: 1) Confusing modulo with integer division (e.g., using a / b instead of a % b), 2) Not handling b=0 (division by zero is undefined), 3) Misunderstanding negative remainders (in most programming languages, a % b has the same sign as a, but for GCD we typically use absolute values), 4) Assuming modulo works the same as mathematical modulus (programming modulo and mathematical modulus differ for negatives). Real code pattern: remainder = a % b

examples.trace:
Step 1: Divide 10 by 3: 10 / 3 = 3 with remainder
Step 2: Compute 3 * 3 = 9
Step 3: Subtract: 10 - 9 = 1
Step 4: Remainder = 1

Step 1: Divide 15 by 5: 15 / 5 = 3 with remainder
Step 2: Compute 5 * 3 = 15
Step 3: Subtract: 15 - 15 = 0
Step 4: Remainder = 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Factors
ladders.desc: Understand what it means for one number to divide another evenly
ladders.explanation: The foundation concept for the brute force GCD algorithm is the idea of integer divisibility: what it means for one integer to divide another evenly. This is a prerequisite from basic arithmetic. The intuition is that if integer d divides integer n evenly (written d | n), then when we divide n by d, we get an integer result with no remainder. In other words, n is a multiple of d. For example, 3 divides 12 evenly because 12 ÷ 3 = 4 exactly, with no leftover. This concept is essential because the GCD is defined as the largest number that divides both input numbers evenly. To find it via brute force, we need to check many potential divisors, and understanding divisibility lets us test each candidate. The algorithm for checking divisibility is: given n and d (d ≠ 0), perform integer division n ÷ d; if the result is an integer (i.e., n % d == 0), then d divides n evenly. Time and space complexity are O(1) for this conceptual check. This foundation connects to the modulo operation (level 4) because modulo gives us a computational way to test divisibility: if n % d == 0, then d divides n. Without grasping this basic concept, the entire GCD problem would be meaningless. Common misunderstandings include: 1) Thinking division always results in a whole number (it doesn't; e.g., 7 ÷ 2 = 3.5), 2) Confusing factors with multiples (factors divide a number, multiples are results of multiplying), 3) Not realizing that 1 divides every integer, and every integer divides itself, 4) Forgetting that division by zero is undefined. Real code pattern: To check if d divides n: n % d == 0

examples.trace:
Step 1: Divide 12 by 4: 12 ÷ 4 = 3
Step 2: Check if result is integer: 3 is an integer
Step 3: Since no remainder, 4 divides 12 evenly
Step 4: Output True

Step 1: Divide 12 by 5: 12 ÷ 5 = 2.4
Step 2: Check if result is integer: 2.4 is not an integer
Step 3: Since there is a remainder, 5 does not divide 12 evenly
Step 4: Output False

approaches.name: Euclidean Algorithm (Subtraction) O(n)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly subtract the smaller number from the larger until both numbers become equal, which is the GCD. This is based on the property that GCD(a,b) = GCD(a-b,b) for a > b. It's more efficient than brute force but slower than the division method.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(a + b) in worst case (e.g., consecutive Fibonacci numbers)

ladders.title: Full Problem — GCD of Two Numbers Using Euclidean Subtraction Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of GCD calculation using repeated subtraction until numbers become equal.
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean subtraction algorithm finds the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two numbers by repeatedly applying the mathematical property: GCD(a,b) = GCD(a-b,b) when a > b. The intuition is that any common divisor of a and b must also divide their difference. By continuously subtracting the smaller number from the larger, we reduce the numbers while preserving their GCD, eventually reaching a point where both numbers are equal, which is the GCD. The algorithm works as follows: 1) Take two numbers a and b. 2) Handle zero inputs first: if a == 0, return b; if b == 0, return a. 3) While a ≠ b: if a > b, set a = a - b; else set b = b - a. 4) Return a (or b) as the GCD. Time complexity is O(a + b) in worst case (e.g., consecutive Fibonacci numbers) because each subtraction reduces the larger number by at least 1. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the subtraction loop. Common mistakes include: not handling negative numbers (should take absolute values), infinite loops if numbers are zero (need special cases), and inefficient implementation for large numbers (use division method instead). Real code pattern: def gcd_subtraction(a, b): a, b = abs(a), abs(b); if a == 0: return b; if b == 0: return a; while a != b: if a > b: a -= b else: b -= a; return a.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Repeated Subtraction Loop for GCD Property
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that repeatedly subtracts smaller number from larger until equality.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the essential iterative process of the Euclidean subtraction algorithm. The intuition is based on the mathematical property: if d divides both a and b, then d also divides a-b. Therefore, by repeatedly replacing the larger number with their difference, we maintain the same GCD while reducing the numbers. The algorithm: 1) Input two non-negative integers a and b. 2) If one input is zero, return the other immediately. 3) While a ≠ b: compare a and b; if a > b, set a = a - b; otherwise set b = b - a. 4) When a = b, that value is the GCD. Time complexity is O(a+b) in worst case as each iteration reduces the sum by at least 1. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 as the main computational engine. Common mistakes: not ensuring numbers are positive (handle negatives before loop), not updating the correct variable, and potential infinite loop if one number is zero (handle separately). Real code pattern: while a != b: if a > b: a -= b else: b -= a.

examples.trace:
Step 1: a=21, b=14, a≠b, a>b → a=21-14=7
Step 2: a=7, b=14, a≠b, b>a → b=14-7=7
Step 3: a=7, b=7, a=b → stop, GCD=7

ladders.title: Core Logic — GCD Property: GCD(a,b) = GCD(a-b,b) for a > b
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the mathematical property that allows subtraction to preserve GCD.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental mathematical insight behind the Euclidean subtraction algorithm: for two positive integers a and b with a > b, GCD(a,b) = GCD(a-b,b). The intuition is that any common divisor d of a and b must also divide their difference a-b because if d|a and d|b, then a = d*m and b = d*n, so a-b = d*(m-n), meaning d|(a-b). Conversely, any common divisor of a-b and b also divides a (since a = (a-b)+b). Therefore, the sets of common divisors are identical, so the greatest common divisor remains unchanged. This property allows us to reduce the problem size while maintaining the solution. The algorithm: given a and b, if a > b, replace a with a-b; otherwise, replace b with b-a. Repeat until a=b. Time complexity depends on how many subtractions are needed. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 as the theoretical basis for the subtraction loop. Common mistakes: misapplying the property when a ≤ b (need to handle both cases), not understanding why it preserves GCD. Real code pattern: if a > b: a = a - b else: b = b - a.

examples.trace: Step 1: a=15, b=10, a>b → apply property: GCD(15,10) = GCD(15-10,10) = GCD(5,10)
Step 1: a=8, b=12, b>a → apply property: GCD(8,12) = GCD(8,12-8) = GCD(8,4)

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing and Subtracting Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Implement logic to compare two numbers and subtract the smaller from the larger.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves the basic operation of comparing two integers and performing subtraction based on the comparison. The intuition is that to apply the GCD property, we need to identify which number is larger and subtract the smaller from it. This requires: 1) Comparison: determine if a > b, a < b, or a = b. 2) Subtraction: compute the difference between the larger and smaller numbers. The algorithm: given a and b, if a > b, set a = a - b; else if b > a, set b = b - a; if a = b, stop. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 as the practical implementation step. Common mistakes: incorrect comparison operators, not handling equality case, subtracting in wrong order. Real code pattern: if a > b: a = a - b elif b > a: b = b - a.

examples.trace:
Step 1: compare a=10 and b=4 → a>b
Step 2: subtract: a = 10 - 4 = 6
Result: a=6, b=4

Step 1: compare a=3 and b=7 → b>a
Step 2: subtract: b = 7 - 3 = 4
Result: a=3, b=4

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Subtraction and Comparison
ladders.desc: Perform subtraction of two integers and compare their values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two basic arithmetic/comparison tasks: 1) Subtraction: given two integers x and y, compute x - y. 2) Comparison: given two integers x and y, determine if x > y, x < y, or x = y. The intuition is that these are primitive operations needed for the Euclidean algorithm. Subtraction finds the difference between numbers, which is used to reduce them while preserving GCD. Comparison determines which number is larger to decide subtraction direction. The algorithm: for subtraction, result = x - y; for comparison, use relational operators. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 as the atomic operations. Common mistakes: integer overflow (not an issue in Python), incorrect order in subtraction, misunderstanding comparison results. Real code pattern: diff = a - b; is_greater = a > b.

examples.trace:
Step 1: subtraction: 10 - 3 = 7
Step 2: comparison: 10 > 3 is true

Step 1: subtraction: 4 - 9 = -5
Step 2: comparison: 4 > 9 is false

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Learn what GCD is and how to find it manually for small numbers.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces the greatest common divisor (GCD), also known as the greatest common factor. The GCD of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without leaving a remainder. Intuition: it represents the biggest number that can evenly divide both numbers, useful in simplifying fractions, number theory, and algorithms. To find GCD manually for small numbers: 1) List all divisors of each number. 2) Identify common divisors. 3) Select the largest common divisor. For example, divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12; divisors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18; common divisors: 1,2,3,6; largest is 6, so GCD(12,18)=6. Time complexity for manual method is O(min(a,b)) to list divisors. Space is O(min(a,b)) to store divisors. This connects to L4 as GCD is what we aim to compute using subtraction. Common mistakes: confusing GCD with LCM, not considering 1 as always a divisor, forgetting negative numbers (GCD is defined as positive). Real code pattern: manually compute by checking divisors from 1 to min(a,b).

examples.trace:
Step 1: divisors of 8: 1,2,4,8
Step 2: divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12
Step 3: common divisors: 1,2,4
Step 4: largest common divisor: 4

Step 1: divisors of 7: 1,7
Step 2: divisors of 5: 1,5
Step 3: common divisors: 1
Step 4: largest common divisor: 1

approaches.name: Euclidean Algorithm (Division/Recursive) O(log(min(a,b)))
approaches.summary: Use recursion or iteration to compute GCD by repeatedly applying GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b) until b becomes 0, then a is the GCD. This is the standard optimal approach due to its logarithmic time complexity and simplicity.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) for iterative, O(log(min(a,b))) for recursive due to call stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute GCD of Two Numbers Using Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the complete Euclidean algorithm to find the greatest common divisor of two integers.
ladders.explanation:
The Euclidean algorithm is an efficient method for computing the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers. The intuition is based on the mathematical property that GCD(a, b) = GCD(b, a mod b). This works because any common divisor of a and b must also divide a mod b (the remainder when a is divided by b). By repeatedly applying this reduction, we shrink the numbers until one becomes zero, at which point the other is the GCD.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Take two integers a and b.
2. While b is not zero:
   - Compute remainder r = a mod b.
   - Set a = b and b = r.
3. When b becomes 0, a contains the GCD.

Time complexity is O(log(min(a, b))) because each step reduces the numbers significantly (at least by half in the worst case). Space complexity is O(1) for iterative implementation.

Connection to L1: This full solution uses the modulo operation repeatedly, which is the key subroutine.

Common mistakes:
- Not handling negative numbers (GCD is defined for absolute values).
- Infinite loop if not updating variables correctly.
- Forgetting to take absolute values.

Real code pattern:
def gcd(a, b):
    a, b = abs(a), abs(b)
    while b:
        a, b = b, a % b
    return a

examples.trace:
Step 1: a=48, b=18, remainder=48%18=12 → a=18, b=12
Step 2: a=18, b=12, remainder=18%12=6 → a=12, b=6
Step 3: a=12, b=6, remainder=12%6=0 → a=6, b=0
b=0, so GCD=6

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Modulo Operation in GCD Reduction
ladders.desc: Implement the modulo operation that drives the Euclidean algorithm's recursive reduction.
ladders.explanation:
The modulo operation (a % b) is the engine of the Euclidean algorithm. It computes the remainder when a is divided by b. The intuition is that this remainder preserves the GCD property: GCD(a, b) = GCD(b, a % b). This works because if d divides both a and b, then a = q*b + r implies r = a - q*b, so d must also divide r. By replacing a with b and b with r, we get smaller numbers while maintaining the same GCD.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Given integers a and b (b ≠ 0).
2. Compute remainder r = a - (a//b)*b, where // is integer division.
3. Return r.

Time complexity is O(1) for basic arithmetic. Space complexity is O(1).

Connection to L0: This modulo operation is repeatedly called in the main GCD loop.

Common mistakes:
- Not handling b=0 case (division by zero).
- Confusing modulo with integer division.
- Incorrect sign handling for negative numbers.

Real code pattern:
def mod(a, b):
    if b == 0: raise ValueError('Division by zero')
    return a - (a//b)*b

ladders.title: Core Logic — GCD Reduction Property: GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b)
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the mathematical property that enables the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental insight behind the Euclidean algorithm. The property states that the GCD of two numbers remains unchanged if we replace the larger number with the remainder when divided by the smaller number. Intuition: Any common divisor d of a and b must divide any linear combination of a and b. Since a mod b = a - q*b (where q = a//b), d must divide the remainder too. Conversely, any divisor of b and (a mod b) must divide a. This creates an invariant that lets us reduce the problem size while preserving the answer.

Step-by-step application:
1. Start with GCD(a, b).
2. If b = 0, answer is a.
3. Otherwise, compute r = a mod b.
4. Problem reduces to GCD(b, r).

Time/space: Conceptual level - no direct complexity.

Connection to L1: This property tells us why we use modulo operation repeatedly.

Common mistakes:
- Applying property incorrectly when b > a (but algorithm handles this by swapping in first iteration).
- Not understanding why property preserves GCD.

Real code pattern:
# This is the recursive formulation:
def gcd(a, b):
    if b == 0: return a
    return gcd(b, a % b)

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Remainders
ladders.desc: Understand how integer division produces quotient and remainder.
ladders.explanation:
Integer division is the mathematical operation that, given two integers a (dividend) and b (divisor, b ≠ 0), produces two results: quotient q and remainder r such that a = b*q + r, where 0 ≤ r < |b|. This is called the division algorithm. The intuition is that we're finding how many whole times b fits into a, and what's left over. The remainder r is always non-negative and less than the divisor's absolute value. This relationship is crucial because the Euclidean algorithm uses the remainder to reduce the problem size.

Step-by-step:
1. For a and b (b ≠ 0).
2. Find q = floor(a/b) (integer division).
3. Compute r = a - b*q.
4. Verify 0 ≤ r < |b|.

Time/space: O(1) for basic arithmetic.

Connection to L2: This division gives us the a mod b needed for the GCD reduction property.

Common mistakes:
- Confusing integer division with floating-point division.
- Forgetting that remainder must be non-negative.
- Not handling negative numbers correctly.

Real code pattern:
def integer_division(a, b):
    q = a // b  # integer division
    r = a - b*q
    return q, r

examples.trace: 17 = 5*3 + 2, so quotient=3, remainder=2
20 = 6*3 + 2, so quotient=3, remainder=2

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparison and Variable Swapping
ladders.desc: Compare two numbers and swap them if needed for algorithm initialization.
ladders.explanation:
Before applying the Euclidean algorithm, we often want to ensure we're working with non-negative numbers and that we handle the case where inputs might be in any order. The intuition is that GCD(a,b) = GCD(|a|,|b|) since GCD is defined for positive numbers, and GCD(a,b) = GCD(b,a) due to commutativity. We take absolute values to handle negatives, and while the algorithm works regardless of order (since first iteration effectively swaps if b > a), explicitly handling this makes the process clearer.

Step-by-step:
1. Take inputs a and b.
2. Replace a with |a| and b with |b| (absolute values).
3. Optionally ensure a ≥ b for clarity (though not strictly necessary).

Time complexity: O(1). Space complexity: O(1).

Connection to L3: This prepares numbers for the division operation.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting to take absolute values for negative inputs.
- Unnecessary swapping that complicates code.
- Not handling zero values properly.

Real code pattern:
def prepare_numbers(a, b):
    a, b = abs(a), abs(b)
    # Optional: if a < b: a, b = b, a
    return a, b

examples.trace: Take absolute values: |-12|=12, |18|=18
Optional swap to ensure a ≥ b: since 5<10, swap to a=10, b=5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Learn what GCD means and find it by listing divisors.
ladders.explanation:
The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without leaving a remainder. Intuition: Think of it as the biggest number that can evenly divide both numbers. For example, GCD(12,18)=6 because 6 divides both 12 and 18, and no larger number does. This is a fundamental concept in number theory with applications in simplifying fractions, cryptography, and algorithm design. To find GCD naively: list all divisors of each number, find common divisors, then take the largest.

Step-by-step naive algorithm:
1. Find all divisors of a.
2. Find all divisors of b.
3. Find common divisors in both lists.
4. Return the largest common divisor.

Time complexity: O(min(a,b)) for naive divisor finding. Space: O(√n) for storing divisors.

Connection to L4: GCD is what we're ultimately computing through the Euclidean algorithm.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting 1 is always a common divisor.
- Not considering negative numbers properly.
- Missing that GCD(0,n)=|n|.

Real code pattern:
def naive_gcd(a, b):
    a, b = abs(a), abs(b)
    if a == 0: return b
    if b == 0: return a
    gcd_val = 1
    for i in range(1, min(a,b)+1):
        if a%i==0 and b%i==0:
            gcd_val = i
    return gcd_val

examples.trace:
Divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12
Divisors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18
Common: 1,2,3,6
Largest: 6

Divisors of 7: 1,7
Divisors of 5: 1,5
Common: 1
Largest: 1

approaches.name: Binary GCD (Stein's Algorithm) O(log(min(a,b)))
approaches.summary: Compute GCD using bitwise operations by handling cases where both numbers are even, one is even, or both are odd, reducing via shifts and subtractions. It's efficient for large integers and avoids modulo operations, useful in low-level or hardware contexts.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute GCD of Two Numbers Using Binary GCD Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the complete Binary GCD (Stein's Algorithm) to find the greatest common divisor of two integers using bitwise operations.
ladders.explanation: The Binary GCD algorithm, also known as Stein's Algorithm, efficiently computes the GCD using bitwise operations instead of modulo operations. The intuition is based on these mathematical properties: (1) gcd(a, 0) = a, (2) if both a and b are even, gcd(a, b) = 2 * gcd(a/2, b/2), (3) if a is even and b is odd, gcd(a, b) = gcd(a/2, b), (4) if both are odd, gcd(a, b) = gcd(|a-b|/2, min(a,b)). This works because dividing even numbers by 2 (right shift) preserves divisibility relationships, and subtracting odd numbers yields an even number that can be halved. The algorithm repeatedly applies these rules until one number becomes zero. Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) because each step reduces the numbers significantly through halving or subtraction. Space is O(1) as it uses only a few variables. This approach avoids expensive modulo operations, making it suitable for large integers or hardware implementations. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the key subroutine of handling odd/even cases. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loops when numbers become equal but not zero (ensure proper termination), and incorrect bit shifting (use >> for division by 2). Real code pattern: def binary_gcd(a, b): a, b = abs(a), abs(b); if a == 0: return b; if b == 0: return a; shift = 0; while ((a | b) & 1) == 0: a >>= 1; b >>= 1; shift += 1; while (a & 1) == 0: a >>= 1; while (b & 1) == 0: b >>= 1; while a != b: if a > b: a = (a - b) >> 1; else: b = (b - a) >> 1; while (a & 1) == 0: a >>= 1; return a << shift;

examples.trace: Step 1: a=56 (even), b=98 (even). Both even, find common power of 2. shift=0. While both even: a>>=1 gives 28, b>>=1 gives 49, shift=1. Now a=28 (even), b=49 (odd). Step 2: While a even: a>>=1 gives 14. a=14 (even): a>>=1 gives 7. Now a=7 (odd), b=49 (odd). Step 3: While b even: b is odd, skip. Step 4: a!=b (7!=49). Since b>a: b=(49-7)>>1 = 42>>1 = 21. b=21 (odd). Step 5: a!=b (7!=21). Since b>a: b=(21-7)>>1 = 14>>1 = 7. b=7 (odd). Step 6: a=b=7. Return a<<shift = 7<<1 = 14.
Step 1: a=0, b=17. Check base cases: if a==0 return b. Directly return 17.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Handle Odd/Even Cases in Binary GCD
ladders.desc: Implement the core reduction step that processes numbers based on their parity (odd/even status).
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the Binary GCD algorithm, determining how to reduce the two numbers based on whether they are odd or even. The intuition leverages these properties: (1) If both numbers are even, they share a factor of 2, so we can factor it out by halving both (right shift) and track the common power of 2. (2) If one is even and the other odd, the even number can be halved because the odd number doesn't have factor 2, so gcd remains unchanged. (3) If both are odd, their difference is even, and we can use gcd(a,b) = gcd((a-b)/2, min(a,b)) after ensuring a > b. This works because subtracting two odd numbers yields an even number, which can then be halved. The algorithm repeatedly applies these rules until the numbers become equal or one becomes zero. Time complexity per call is O(log n) in worst case due to halving, but amortized over the full algorithm. Space is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called iteratively in the main algorithm to reduce the problem size. Connection to L2: It relies on the core insight of parity-based reduction. Common mistakes include not updating both numbers correctly in the both-even case, or incorrectly handling the subtraction when both are odd (must take absolute difference and halve). Real code pattern: def reduce_gcd(a, b): while True: if a == 0: return b; if b == 0: return a; if (a & 1) == 0 and (b & 1) == 0: return 2 * reduce_gcd(a>>1, b>>1); elif (a & 1) == 0: return reduce_gcd(a>>1, b); elif (b & 1) == 0: return reduce_gcd(a, b>>1); else: if a > b: return reduce_gcd((a-b)>>1, b); else: return reduce_gcd((b-a)>>1, a);

examples.trace: Step 1: a=12 (even), b=18 (even). Both even: return 2*reduce_gcd(6,9). Step 2: a=6 (even), b=9 (odd). a even: return reduce_gcd(3,9). Step 3: a=3 (odd), b=9 (odd). Both odd, a<b: return reduce_gcd((9-3)>>1,3) = reduce_gcd(3,3). Step 4: a=3, b=3. Both odd, a=b: return a=3. Backtrack: Step 3 returns 3, Step 2 returns 3, Step 1 returns 2*3=6.
Step 1: a=5 (odd), b=10 (even). b even: return reduce_gcd(5,5). Step 2: a=5, b=5. Both odd, a=b: return 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Parity-Based Reduction for GCD
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the mathematical rules for reducing GCD based on whether numbers are odd or even.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight behind Binary GCD: using parity (odd/even) to simplify the GCD computation. The intuition is rooted in number theory: (1) If both numbers are even, they share a factor of 2, so gcd(a,b) = 2 * gcd(a/2, b/2). This is because 2 is a common divisor, and dividing by 2 reduces the problem size while preserving the GCD up to that factor. (2) If one number is even and the other odd, the even number can be halved without affecting the GCD because the odd number has no factor of 2, so gcd(a,b) = gcd(a/2, b) when a is even and b odd (or vice versa). (3) If both are odd, their difference is even, and we use gcd(a,b) = gcd((a-b)/2, min(a,b)) for a > b. This works because gcd(a,b) = gcd(a-b, b) (standard Euclidean property), and since a-b is even, we can halve it. The algorithm applies these rules recursively until base cases (one number zero). Time complexity per reduction is O(1), but overall O(log n) due to repeated halving. Space is O(log n) if implemented recursively, O(1) iteratively. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the subroutine. Connection to L3: It builds upon understanding of even/odd properties. Common mistakes include misapplying the rule when both are odd (must ensure to halve the difference, not the numbers), or not handling the both-even case correctly (must track the common power of 2). Real code pattern: Focus on rules: if both even: gcd = 2*gcd(a/2,b/2); if a even, b odd: gcd = gcd(a/2,b); if a odd, b even: gcd = gcd(a,b/2); if both odd: gcd = gcd((abs(a-b))/2, min(a,b)).

examples.trace: Apply rules: Step 1: Both even (20,30) -> gcd = 2*gcd(10,15). Step 2: a=10 even, b=15 odd -> gcd = gcd(5,15). Step 3: a=5 odd, b=15 odd -> both odd, a<b: gcd = gcd((15-5)/2,5) = gcd(5,5). Step 4: Both odd and equal -> gcd=5. Backtrack: Step 1 gives 2*5=10.

Step 1: a=9 odd, b=12 even -> b even: gcd = gcd(9,6). Step 2: a=9 odd, b=6 even -> b even: gcd = gcd(9,3). Step 3: a=9 odd, b=3 odd -> both odd, a>b: gcd = gcd((9-3)/2,3) = gcd(3,3). Step 4: Both odd and equal -> gcd=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Properties of Even and Odd Numbers in Division
ladders.desc: Learn how even and odd numbers behave under division and subtraction in the context of GCD.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept explores the mathematical properties of even and odd numbers that underpin the Binary GCD algorithm. The intuition is that even numbers are divisible by 2, while odd numbers are not, and this affects how we can simplify GCD computations. Key properties: (1) An even number can be written as 2k, an odd number as 2k+1. (2) If both numbers are even, they share a factor of 2, so dividing both by 2 (right shift) reduces the problem while preserving the GCD up to that factor: gcd(2a, 2b) = 2 * gcd(a, b). (3) If one number is even and the other odd, the even number can be divided by 2 without changing the GCD because the odd number has no factor of 2: gcd(2a, b) = gcd(a, b) for odd b. This works because any common divisor must divide both numbers, and since b is odd, it doesn't have 2 as a factor, so the factor 2 in the even number is irrelevant to the GCD. (4) Subtracting two odd numbers yields an even number: (2k+1) - (2m+1) = 2(k-m), which is even and can be halved. This leads to gcd(a,b) = gcd((a-b)/2, min(a,b)) for odd a,b. Understanding these properties is crucial for designing the reduction steps. Time and space are not directly applicable here as it's conceptual. Connection to L2: These properties justify the parity-based reduction rules. Connection to L4: It relies on basic operations like division by 2 and subtraction. Common mistakes include assuming these properties apply to all cases (e.g., dividing an odd number by 2 loses information) or not considering absolute values in subtraction. Real code pattern: Conceptual understanding: even: n%2==0 or n&1==0; odd: n%2==1 or n&1==1. Use these to apply properties in algorithms.

examples.trace: 8 = 2*4, 12 = 2*6. Both even, so gcd(8,12) = 2*gcd(4,6). Then 4 and 6 are even: 4=2*2, 6=2*3, so gcd(4,6)=2*gcd(2,3). Now 2 even, 3 odd: gcd(2,3)=gcd(1,3) since 2 even can be halved. Then gcd(1,3)=1. Backtrack: gcd(4,6)=2*1=2, gcd(8,12)=2*2=4.
7 and 11 are odd. Their difference: 11-7=4, which is even. Then gcd(7,11) = gcd(4,7) since gcd(a,b)=gcd(a-b,b). But 4 is even, so can be halved: gcd(4,7)=gcd(2,7). 2 even, 7 odd: gcd(2,7)=gcd(1,7)=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise Shifts and Parity Checks
ladders.desc: Perform right shifts to divide by 2 and use bitwise AND to check if a number is even or odd.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using bitwise operators to efficiently handle division by 2 and parity determination, which are key in Binary GCD. The intuition is that in binary representation, dividing by 2 corresponds to shifting bits right by one position (>> 1), and checking if a number is even or odd can be done by examining the least significant bit (LSB) using AND with 1. Specifically: (1) Right shift (>>): For a positive integer n, n >> 1 equals floor(n/2). This is because shifting bits right drops the LSB, effectively dividing by 2 and discarding any remainder. For example, binary 1010 (10 decimal) >> 1 gives 0101 (5 decimal). (2) Parity check: n & 1 returns 1 if n is odd (LSB is 1), 0 if even (LSB is 0). This works because AND with 1 isolates the LSB. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space is O(1). These operations are faster than arithmetic division and modulo, which is why Binary GCD is efficient. Connection to L3: These operations implement the properties of even/odd numbers. Connection to L5: They build upon binary representation. Common mistakes include using right shift on negative numbers (which may have implementation-defined behavior; always take absolute values first), or confusing >> with << (left shift multiplies by 2). Also, ensure to use integer shifts. Real code pattern: To divide by 2: n = n >> 1. To check if even: if (n & 1) == 0. To check if odd: if (n & 1) == 1. To multiply by 2: n = n << 1.

examples.trace: 10 in binary: 1010. Right shift by 1: 1010 >> 1 = 0101, which is 5 in decimal. AND with 1: 1010 & 0001 = 0000, so result 0, indicating even.
7 in binary: 0111. Right shift by 1: 0111 >> 1 = 0011, which is 3 in decimal. AND with 1: 0111 & 0001 = 0001, so result 1, indicating odd.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Representation and Even/Odd Numbers
ladders.desc: Understand how numbers are represented in binary and what makes a number even or odd.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding binary number representation and the definition of even and odd numbers. In binary, numbers are expressed using bits (0s and 1s), where each bit represents a power of 2. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*2^2 + 0*2^1 + 1*2^0). The least significant bit (LSB, the rightmost bit) determines parity: if LSB is 0, the number is even; if LSB is 1, the number is odd. This is because even numbers are multiples of 2, so in binary, they end with 0 (e.g., 2=10, 4=100), while odd numbers have a remainder of 1 when divided by 2, so they end with 1 (e.g., 3=11, 7=111). Intuitively, dividing by 2 in binary corresponds to shifting all bits right by one position, which drops the LSB. This foundational knowledge is crucial for Binary GCD because the algorithm uses bitwise operations to check parity (via LSB) and divide by 2 (via right shift). Time and space are not applicable as this is basic math. Connection to L4: This explains why bitwise operations work for parity checks and division by 2. Common mistakes include confusing binary with decimal representation, or not understanding that LSB is the rightmost bit. Real code pattern: Conceptual: to check if n is even, see if n % 2 == 0 or in binary, if last bit is 0. To divide by 2, compute n // 2 or in binary, shift bits right.

examples.trace: 6 in decimal. Convert to binary: 6 = 4+2 = 1*2^2 + 1*2^1 + 0*2^0, so binary 110. LSB is 0, so it's even. Check: 6 divided by 2 is 3 with remainder 0, confirming even.
9 in decimal. Convert to binary: 9 = 8+1 = 1*2^3 + 0*2^2 + 0*2^1 + 1*2^0, so binary 1001. LSB is 1, so it's odd. Check: 9 divided by 2 is 4 with remainder 1, confirming odd.
